headline: GOVERNMENT LAUNCHES MASSIVE SELF-RELIANCE DRIVE, NEW FACILITY IN MADHYA PRADESH TO BOOST INDIGENOUS DEFENCE PRODUCTION
summary: India is currently renewing its self-reliance drive, with efforts to increase indigenous defence production and manufacturing. A new facility in Madhya Pradesh will house the entire missile manufacturing value chain. The government is also promoting homegrown technologies and discussing ways to strengthen indigenous production with industry representatives. Thousands of jobs are expected to be created through these initiatives.

sections.paragraph: The concept of Atmanirbhar Bharat, or Self-Reliant India, has its roots in discussions of national security, poverty alleviation, and the Digital India initiative, with Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi first using the term 'self-reliance' in 2014. The phrase gained prominence in 2020 with the announcement of India's economic stimulus package in response to the COVID-19 pandemic. Despite government initiatives, the manufacturing sector's contribution to annual GDP growth declined significantly from 2015 to 2024, with its share of GDP falling to a historic low of 13% in 2024, comparable to levels last seen in 1967. The Atmanirbhar Bharat policy framework has been linked to previous initiatives, such as the Make in India movement, with some viewing it as a re-packaged version. The government has announced several Atmanirbhar Bharat packages, worth ₹29.87 trillion, in response to the COVID-19 crisis, aiming to promote self-reliance and reduce dependence on imports. The concept has also been connected to India's economic dependence on China and efforts to promote domestic production and exports.

The Quadrilateral Security Dialogue, comprising India, the US, Japan, and Australia, has gained momentum as a counterbalance to China's growing aggression in the Indo-pacific region. However, the group's capacity to provide an alternative to China in trade is currently limited, with China remaining a crucial hub of global value chains. India, Japan, the US, and Australia are all dependent on supplies from China for manufacturing products, and India's own dependence on China is significant. Economists suggest that while it will be difficult for India to decouple from China in the short run, the country has the potential to give China a run for its money in the long run, particularly if it undertakes deeper domestic policy reforms to improve ease of doing business and infrastructure. India's low-cost production capabilities make it a potential competitor to China, with some experts noting that it is the only country other than China that can produce at a similar scale and price. The Quad countries' growing recognition of the need to economically decouple from China may also work in India's favor over time.

sources.title: Can India replace China as the world’s factory?

sections.paragraph: The business impact of India's defence sector is significant, with the government's 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' and 'Make in India' initiatives aiming to reduce dependence on defence imports and boost indigenous production. Companies such as Hindustan Aeronautics and Bharat Electronics are expected to benefit from these initiatives, with Hindustan Aeronautics being debt-free and having a strong dividend payout ratio. The sector is also seeing investment in new facilities, such as a Rs 2,500 crore missile manufacturing facility in Madhya Pradesh, which will create thousands of jobs and complement existing defence manufacturing. Additionally, foreign companies like Embraer are partnering with Indian companies to manufacture and maintain aircraft, aligning with national initiatives to boost India's aerospace capabilities. The growth of the defence sector is driven by national security needs and is expected to have a positive impact on the economy. Top defence stocks, including Avantel Soft, are playing a major role in reducing Indian defence imports and increasing exports.
